Patch Set 1:

Under what SELinux policy did you run this test? Did you use any additional policy modules? Since this test checks for the enforcing mode, I believe, it will not work under the reference policy, since lfs/lctl/lst/loadgen/etc require specific permissions.

Would you like to add policy specific tests, e.g. those that check different patterns of access and security labels of newly created objects?

(4 comments)

(1 comment)

Patch Set 1:

> Under what SELinux policy did you run this test? Did you use any
 > additional policy modules?

We do not use any additional policy module. The tests run with the standard SELinux policy.

Patch Set 2:

(1 comment)

Let's see if sanity-selinux can run in the autotest environment.
Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 2:

> We do not use any additional policy module. The tests run with the standard SELinux policy.

Could you please explain what you mean by  the standard SELinux policy?

RedHat 6 SELinux policy packages are based on the reference policy.

Simply running "bash llmount.sh" under the MLS policy will fail the security checks at least in the following fashion:

type=1400 audit(1441735642.518:168): avc:  denied  { read write } for  pid=17271 comm="lctl" name="lnet" dev=devtmpfs ino=61013 scontext=root:sysadm_r:sysadm_t:s0-s15:c0.c1023 tcontext=system_u:object_r:device_t:s0 tclass=chr_file
type=1400 audit(1441735642.518:168): avc:  denied  { open } for  pid=17271 comm="lctl" name="lnet" dev=devtmpfs ino=61013 scontext=root:sysadm_r:sysadm_t:s0-s15:c0.c1023 tcontext=system_u:object_r:device_t:s0 tclass=chr_file

I believe, without security modules, it's not possible to use t-f. Unless from some broken unconfined domain.
Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 2:

> > We do not use any additional policy module. The tests run with
 > the standard SELinux policy.
 > 
 > Could you please explain what you mean by  the standard SELinux
 > policy?
 > 
 > RedHat 6 SELinux policy packages are based on the reference policy.
 > 
 > Simply running "bash llmount.sh" under the MLS policy will fail the
 > security checks at least in the following fashion:
 > 

We do not use MLS. We run with the targeted policy, which is the default one in RHEL7. With this policy we do not need any additional policy module.

Patch Set 2:

I believe, most Lustre installations are multi-user HPC environments and not just simple web/ftp/whatever servers. It seems to be of little value to assume that Lustre works only with targeted policy.

Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1

(5 comments)

The patch looks fine in general, but please answer the questions from the review. Thanks.

Patch Set 2:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 2:

(4 comments)

Thanks for your comments Andrew. I have replied to you inline, but I am not sure I will be able to change the code before next week.

Regarding targeted and MLS policies, what I meant is that I expect to run this sanity-selinux test with targeted policy as a first step, as it enables to check proper extended attribute processing.

Patch Set 6: Code-Review-1

(19 comments)

I can't comment about the SELinux tests, but some minor changes needed to the test script to make it match Lustre script coding style, see:

  http://wiki.lustre.org/Lustre_Script_Coding_Style

Also, this test didn't actually get run in our test environment because SELinux was disabled.  It seems we need to enable SELinux before this test will be valid.

While I'm not against landing the patch as-is, there is currently no way to know if it is passing, nor if there are regressions.

parents.subject: LU-8369 nodemap: ignore loopback NID when classifying

commit.subject: LU-5560 tests: add sanity-selinux.sh
commit.message:
LU-5560 tests: add sanity-selinux.sh

New test sanity-selinux.sh aims at exercing SELinux support
on the client side, as implemented according to LU-5560.
This patch also adds new fail_locs in CLIO.

Test-Parameters: trivial testlist=sanity-selinux
Signed-off-by: Sebastien Buisson <sebastien.buisson@bull.net>
